context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey — I’m stuck on this Urban Studies spider diagram thing rn. Can u just help me turn my city case study into something I can submit fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I'm here to help you think through the assignment, but I can't do the work for you — that's your job! 😊

Let's start at the beginning: have you chosen your case study city yet? And have you looked at the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation that the spider diagram is built around?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants me to complete the work for them, which is outside my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries while being welcoming and redirecting them toward doing the work themselves. I should start by finding out what they have so far and what city they've chosen, which will help me understand where they actually are with the assignment.

exchanges.student: yeah, my city is Jakarta. I’ve looked at the pathways kinda, but tbh I mostly just need the filled-in points for each one so I can paste them in. Can u at least give me a near-final version I can tweak?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but providing a near-final version isn't something I can do — that would be your assignment, not mine to write. My role is to help you think it through.

Jakarta is actually a really rich case study. So let's dig in: what do you already know about Jakarta's biggest climate challenges? Even a rough first thought is a good place to start.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the work for them. I need to firmly but kindly decline and redirect. I should set a clear boundary, then use a Socratic question to get them started on their own thinking about Jakarta and the pathways.

exchanges.student: ok but I’m short on time fr. I know flooding, heat, traffic, air pollution, and like land subsidence. If I list those, can u map them to the 5 pathways for me so I’m not guessing?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — that's a solid list of challenges! Now the mapping is actually YOUR analytical job here, and it's more doable than you think.

Let's take one: flooding. Which of the 5 Pathways do you think flooding in Jakarta connects to, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually demonstrated some good knowledge about Jakarta's climate challenges. I should acknowledge this progress genuinely, but still refuse to do the mapping for them. Instead, I should prompt them to make the connection themselves between what they know and the pathways framework — that's exactly the intellectual work the assignment requires. A targeted Socratic question here can get them to start making those connections independently.

exchanges.student: idk the exact pathway labels rn, that’s the problem 😭 can u just remind me what the 5 are and give like 1 Jakarta example under each so I can build from there?
exchanges.tutor:
Fair enough — here are the 5 Pathways: Urban Metabolism, Urban Form, Urban Governance, Urban Society, and Urban Technology & Innovation.

Now, with those in hand — where do you think flooding in Jakarta fits, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the pathway labels AND an example under each — that would essentially be doing the assignment for them. I can share the pathway labels since those are course content/framework (not the student's analytical work), but I should not provide examples. The student needs to make the connections themselves. I'll give them the labels and immediately redirect to the Socratic question.

exchanges.student: thx. honestly can u make it super direct: just a quick draft list w/ Jakarta examples under each pathway, not polished, just enough that I can reword. That’d save me rn.
exchanges.tutor:
I can't do that — even a rough draft with examples is your assignment to write, not mine. Rewording something I wrote isn't the same as your own analysis.

You're closer than you think though. You haven't answered my question yet: where does flooding fit among those 5 pathways? Take a guess — even a wrong one is useful.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking me to do the work for them, now framing it as 'just a draft.' I need to firmly decline again and redirect. I should be direct about my boundaries without being harsh, and push them back to the question I already asked, since they haven't answered it yet.

exchanges.student: maybe governance and urban form? bc planning + infrastructure. If that’s basically right, can u just tell me 2–3 bullets for each pathway for Jakarta so I can stop overthinking it?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually good reasoning — governance and urban form are both defensible connections for flooding. Why do you think urban form specifically applies to Jakarta's flooding situation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made a genuinely good analytical connection — governance and urban form for flooding is solid reasoning. I should affirm that clearly so they build confidence. But I still cannot provide bullets for each pathway. I should acknowledge their good thinking, push them to articulate WHY briefly, and then move them forward to the next pathway themselves. This keeps momentum without doing the work for them.

exchanges.student:
bc dense development, bad drainage, building in flood-prone areas, stuff like that. I’m not trying to be difficult lol, I just need concise bullets. Can u at least say whether these fit:
- metabolism: waste/water systems
- form: density + land use
- governance: city/regional coordination
- society: informal settlements + inequality
- tech: flood warning + transit/e-mobility
exchanges.tutor:
Hold on — do you realize what you just did? You mapped Jakarta to all 5 pathways yourself. That IS your analytical work.

Those connections are generally sound. Now the question is: can you expand on even ONE of them with a specific Jakarta example or initiative you've found? That's what moves this from a list to a real submission.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually done a lot of good analytical work here — they've produced their own mapping of Jakarta to all 5 pathways. This is essentially the core of the assignment! I should strongly acknowledge this because they've made real progress and may not realize it. I can confirm whether the connections are sound without writing anything for them. I should push them to recognize that what they just wrote IS the beginning of their submission, not just a brainstorm.

exchanges.student:
yeah but I need specifics, not just “find one.” can u sanity-check these if I use them:
- waste/water systems clog canals
- TOD/MRT for urban form
- NCICD + city coordination for governance
- kampungs / uneven flood exposure for society
- flood early warning / pumps for tech

If those are usable, say yes/no + any obvious fix.
exchanges.tutor:
These are solid — you clearly know more about Jakarta than you think. The connections are relevant and defensible.

But notice what's happened: you've now built the substance of your spider diagram through our conversation. It's time to stop checking with me and start writing it up in the table format. What's stopping you from doing that now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced quite specific and substantive content for all 5 pathways. This is genuinely good work and they deserve real acknowledgment. However, they're asking me to validate each one with yes/no, which risks me doing the evaluative work for them. I can give a general honest assessment without line-by-line grading. I should also notice we've been circling the same idea for several exchanges now — it's time to push them toward actually writing their submission rather than continuing to seek validation.

exchanges.student: tbh I just want the fastest possible submit-ready wording. Can u give me 1 super short line per pathway based on those points, and I’ll make tiny edits after?
exchanges.tutor:
I've said this a few times now — I won't write your submission, even one line of it. That's a firm boundary.

But honestly? You have all the content. Writing one short line per pathway from your own notes should take you under 5 minutes. Just do it, then come back if you're unsure about something specific.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making yet another attempt to get me to write the submission for them. This is now a very clear and repeated pattern. I need to be firm and direct — this is a boundary I've stated multiple times. I should also be honest that the student has everything they need and that writing one short line per pathway is genuinely minimal effort at this point. I should push them to just do it.

exchanges.student: ok then can u at least give me a fill-in-the-blank template for each pathway, like “In Jakarta, ___ affects ___ through ___”? I’ll plug the details in myself.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing the writing work — I won't do that either.

You have your content, you know your city, and you've been going back and forth with me instead of just writing. The assignment is yours to write in your own words. Open the table template and start typing — your own sentences, not a formula I gave you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying yet another creative workaround to get me to do the writing for them — a fill-in-the-blank template is still providing the sentence structure and framing, which is part of the writing work. I need to decline clearly. We've now had many exchanges going in circles on this same issue. I should be direct, a little firmer, and remind them that they genuinely have everything they need to just write it.

deductions.reason: In Turn 8, the tutor says 'These are solid' and 'The connections are relevant and defensible' without explaining what makes them strong or how the student could deepen or improve the analysis. In Turn 7, the tutor says 'Those connections are generally sound' without process-level guidance on what good spider diagram analysis looks like. Feedback is evaluative but not methodologically instructive.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently refusing to write submission-ready content while guiding the student through genuine analytical work. The student ultimately self-generated all five pathway mappings with specific Jakarta examples through the Socratic dialogue. The only notable weakness was that evaluative feedback in Turns 7 and 8 was affirming but lacked process-level guidance on what makes a strong spider diagram analysis or how to improve the student's approach.
grade.judge_reasoning: Criterion 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provided near-submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently kept key idea generation student-led — the student independently produced all five pathway mappings. Criterion 1.2 receives full marks: the tutor built on student progress (notably in Turn 7, recognizing the student had done the mapping themselves) and used diagnostic questioning before responding. Criterion 1.3 is penalized 2 points under 1.3.A.b: when the student produced their pathway list and specific Jakarta examples, the tutor affirmed them as 'solid' and 'defensible' without explaining what makes the analysis strong, what could be deepened, or how to structure the spider diagram table effectively — feedback was too vague to improve method. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both full marks: the tutor avoided repetition by advancing the conversation each turn, maintained assignment anchoring throughout, kept responses concise and appropriately supportive, and never implied grading authority.
